content:
# Revelation Chapter 10 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus, pages 284-285 | **Source Image:** REV_010_page_001.jpg, REV_010_page_002.jpg

1. And I saw another mighty angel coming down out of heaven, clothed with a cloud, and the rainbow was upon his head, and his face was like the sun, and his feet like pillars of fire.

2. And he had in his hand a little scroll that had been opened. And he set his right foot upon the sea and his left upon the land,

3. and he cried out with a loud voice, as a lion roars. And when he cried out, the seven thunders uttered their own voices.

4. And when the seven thunders had spoken, I was about to write; and I heard a voice from heaven saying, "Seal up the things which the seven thunders spoke, and do not write them."

5. And the angel whom I saw standing upon the sea and upon the land raised his right hand to heaven,

6. and swore by him who lives forever and ever, who created the heaven and the things in it, and the earth and the things in it, and the sea and the things in it, that there shall be delay no longer,

7. but in the days of the voice of the seventh angel, when he is about to sound his trumpet, then the mystery of God is completed, as he proclaimed the good news to his servants the prophets.

8. And the voice which I heard from heaven was again speaking with me and saying, "Go, take the scroll which is opened in the hand of the angel who stands upon the sea and upon the land."

9. And I went to the angel, telling him to give me the little scroll. And he says to me, "Take it and eat it up, and it shall make your stomach bitter, but in your mouth it shall be sweet as honey."

10. And I took the little scroll out of the hand of the angel and ate it up, and it was in my mouth sweet as honey; and when I had eaten it, my stomach was made bitter.

11. And they say to me, "You must prophesy again concerning many peoples and nations and tongues and kings."

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

**Verse 2:** All three translators agree on the core image — the angel holds a small, opened scroll and plants one foot on the sea, one on the land. Minor differences in phrasing are noted in the disagreement log.

**Verse 4:** All three agree on the essential content — John is commanded to seal up and not write what the seven thunders said.

**Verse 5:** All three render the angel raising his right hand to heaven with consistent wording.

**Verse 10:** All three agree on the sequence: sweet as honey in the mouth, bitter in the stomach/belly.

---
## Disagreement Log

**Verse 1 — "strong" vs. "mighty"; "heaven" vs. "the heaven"; "feet" vs. "legs":**
- EMET: "another strong angel coming down out of the heaven...his face [was] as the sun, and his feet as pillars of fire"
- LOGOS: "another mighty angel coming down out of heaven...his face was like the sun, and his feet like pillars of fire"
- PNEUMA: "another mighty angel coming down from heaven...His face blazed like the sun, and his legs were like pillars of fire"
- *Resolution:* SOPHIA adopts "mighty" (LOGOS/PNEUMA majority) over "strong" (EMET) for ischyron — both are valid, but "mighty" better conveys angelic grandeur. "Feet" is retained over "legs" (PNEUMA) — the Greek podes means feet specifically. "Like the sun" follows LOGOS; PNEUMA's "blazed like" is interpretive embellishment.

**Verse 1 — "having been clothed with" vs. "clothed with" vs. "wrapped in":**
- EMET: "having been clothed with a cloud"
- LOGOS: "clothed with a cloud"
- PNEUMA: "wrapped in a cloud"
- *Resolution:* SOPHIA adopts "clothed with a cloud" (LOGOS) — it preserves the peribeblemenon sense without the over-literal participial construction (EMET) or the interpretive "wrapped" (PNEUMA).

**Verse 2 — "little scroll having been opened" vs. "little scroll that had been opened" vs. "small scroll that lay open":**
- EMET: "a little scroll having been opened"
- LOGOS: "a little scroll that had been opened"
- PNEUMA: "a small scroll that lay open in his hand"
- *Resolution:* SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "that had been opened" captures the perfect participle naturally. PNEUMA's "lay open" is more interpretive. "Little scroll" is preferred over "small scroll" (PNEUMA) to preserve the diminutive biblaridion consistently.

**Verse 2 — "placed" vs. "set" vs. "planted":**
- EMET: "he placed his right foot"
- LOGOS: "he set his right foot"
- PNEUMA: "He planted his right foot"
- *Resolution:* SOPHIA adopts "set" (LOGOS) — a neutral, faithful rendering. "Planted" (PNEUMA) adds connotations of authority not explicit in the Greek etheken. "Placed" (EMET) is acceptable but less natural.

**Verse 3 — "great voice" vs. "loud voice" vs. "powerful voice"; "spoke" vs. "uttered" vs. "responded":**
- EMET: "cried out with a great voice as a lion roars...the seven thunders spoke [with] their own voices"
- LOGOS: "cried out with a loud voice, as a lion roars...the seven thunders uttered their own voices"
- PNEUMA: "shouted with a voice as powerful as a lion's roar...the seven thunders responded with their own voices"
- *Resolution:* SOPHIA adopts "loud voice" (LOGOS) for phone megale — standard rendering. "Uttered their own voices" (LOGOS) is preferred over "spoke" (EMET, less precise) and "responded" (PNEUMA, which implies a dialogic relationship not explicit in the Greek elalēsan).

**Verse 6 — "time shall be no longer" vs. "delay no longer" vs. "no more delay":**
- EMET: "that time shall be no longer"
- LOGOS: "that there shall be delay no longer"
- PNEUMA: "There will be no more delay!"
- *Resolution:* SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "delay no longer" correctly interprets chronos in this context as referring to an interval of waiting, not the abstract concept of time (EMET). PNEUMA captures the same meaning but with dynamic restructuring.

**Verse 7 — "is finished" vs. "is completed" vs. "will be fulfilled"; "proclaimed the good news" vs. "announced":**
- EMET: "the mystery of God is finished, as he proclaimed [the good news] to his own servants the prophets"
- LOGOS: "the mystery of God is completed, as he proclaimed the good news to his servants the prophets"
- PNEUMA: "God's hidden plan will be fulfilled, just as he announced to his servants the prophets"
- *Resolution:* SOPHIA adopts "is completed" (LOGOS) for etelesthē — the prophetic aorist rendered as present. "Finished" (EMET) is acceptable but less precise for teleo. PNEUMA's "hidden plan" interprets mysterion rather than translating it. "Proclaimed the good news" (EMET/LOGOS) is retained for euēngelisen over PNEUMA's "announced," preserving the gospel-proclamation overtone.

**Verse 8 — "out of the heaven" vs. "from heaven"; "which has been opened" vs. "which is opened" vs. "the open scroll":**
- EMET: "the voice which I heard out of the heaven...the scroll which has been opened"
- LOGOS: "the voice which I heard from heaven...the scroll which is opened"
- PNEUMA: "the voice from heaven...the open scroll"
- *Resolution:* SOPHIA follows LOGOS throughout. "From heaven" is natural English for ek tou ouranou. "Which is opened" preserves the Greek perfect participle in accessible form.

**Verse 9 — "saying to him to give me" vs. "telling him to give me" vs. "asked him to give me"; "belly" vs. "stomach":**
- EMET: "saying to him to give me the little scroll...it shall make your belly bitter"
- LOGOS: "telling him to give me the little scroll...it shall make your stomach bitter"
- PNEUMA: "asked him to give me the small scroll...It will turn your stomach sour"
- *Resolution:* SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "telling him" is natural for legōn with indirect speech. "Stomach" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) over "belly" (EMET) for koilia in formal English. PNEUMA's "turn your stomach sour" is interpretive; "make your stomach bitter" (LOGOS) is faithful to pikranei.

**Verse 11 — "concerning peoples and nations and tongues and many kings" vs. "concerning many peoples..." vs. "about many peoples...":**
- EMET: "concerning peoples and nations and tongues and many kings"
- LOGOS: "concerning many peoples and nations and tongues and kings"
- PNEUMA: "about many peoples, nations, languages, and kings"
- *Resolution:* SOPHIA follows LOGOS for the placement of "many" (pollois modifies the entire series, placed first for flow). "Tongues" is retained over "languages" (PNEUMA) to preserve the traditional biblical register of glōssais. "Concerning" (EMET/LOGOS) over "about" (PNEUMA) for the more formal preposition epi.

---

*Signed, SOPHIA — in wisdom, seeking the faithful center where precision meets clarity*
